name: IEEE ICICLE — Industry Consortium on Learning Engineering
description:
Professional standards body for learning engineering. Produces the LE Adoption Maturity Model, competency frameworks, annual conference proceedings, and the Journal of Learning Engineering. Chartered 2017 under IEEE LTSC.
The institutional anchor of the modern LE field, providing the primary community infrastructure, shared vocabulary, and standards work that distinguish learning engineering as a professional discipline.
notes: Chartered 2017 under IEEE LTSC; convener of the annual LE conference.

name: The Simon Initiative
description:
Cross-disciplinary learning engineering ecosystem at CMU named for Herbert Simon. Encompasses LearnLab, OLI, DataShop/LearnSphere, METALS master's program, and the OpenSimon Toolkit. Hundreds of affiliated faculty collaborate across schools to design, instrument, and iteratively improve learning systems using rigorous data.
The world's most integrated LE research-to-practice ecosystem, housing the world's largest open educational log data bank and serving as the primary institutional model for what learning engineering looks like at institutional scale.
notes: Named for Herbert A. Simon; umbrella for LearnLab, OLI, DataShop/LearnSphere, METALS, and OpenSimon Toolkit.

name: Society for Learning Analytics Research (SoLAR)
description:
International academic society promoting learning analytics research, practice, and policy. Organizes the annual LAK (Learning Analytics and Knowledge) conference and the Learning Analytics Summer Institute (LASI). Publishes the Journal of Learning Analytics. Founded 2011 alongside the first LAK conference.
Emphasizes practice-oriented, ethical analytics distinct from pure EDM, with strong attention to equity, transparency, and the socio-technical implications of deploying analytics in educational contexts.
notes: Founded 2011; organizes LAK conference and LASI; publishes Journal of Learning Analytics.

name: Penn Center for Learning Analytics
description:
Research center at the University of Pennsylvania directing educational data mining and learning engineering policy work, led by Ryan Baker. Produced the influential 'High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering' report (2021), which mapped priority areas for the field.
Baker serves as editor of the Journal of Educational Data Mining and is a primary organizer of the EDM conference. The center bridges computational EDM methods with applied learning engineering, making it a key node in the national LE policy conversation.
notes: Ryan Baker, PI; produced 'High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering' (2021).

name: HCII — Human-Computer Interaction Institute, Carnegie Mellon
description:
Academic home of learning sciences and technologies at CMU. Houses Ken Koedinger, Al Corbett, and the PACT Center. Offers PhD and MS programs integrating HCI, learning sciences, and data science. Co-originator of learning engineering as a named field.
Home of DataShop, CTAT (Cognitive Tutor Authoring Tools), and the Cognitive Tutor research lineage — among the most replicated bodies of evidence in applied learning science. The HCII is the primary academic source of the methodological and theoretical frameworks that define learning engineering as a discipline.
notes: Houses Koedinger, Corbett, and the PACT Center; co-originator of learning engineering as a named discipline.

name: International Educational Data Mining Society (IEDMS)
description:
Society organizing the annual EDM (Educational Data Mining) conference and publishing the Journal of Educational Data Mining. Focuses on computational, discovery-oriented analysis of educational data, with emphasis on mathematical and algorithmic approaches to knowledge tracing, student modeling, and behavioral prediction.
EDM is complementary to but distinct from learning analytics: EDM prioritizes algorithmic rigor and discovery, while LA emphasizes socio-technical implications and practice. Both communities are essential to the learning engineering toolkit.
notes: Organizes annual EDM conference; publishes JEDM; sister community to SoLAR/LAK.

name: Learning Sciences Research Institute, UIC
description:
Interdisciplinary research institute at the University of Illinois Chicago spanning learning sciences, computational methods, and educational equity. Conducts research that bridges theoretical learning science and applied educational intervention, with particular attention to equity and access in urban educational contexts.
LSRI provides a model for LE research that integrates rigorous methodology with a commitment to socially situated, equity-centered approaches — an important counterweight to purely technocentric conceptions of the field.

name: IEEE ICICLE SIGs & MIGs
description:
Official listing of IEEE ICICLE Special Interest Groups (SIGs) and Market Interest Groups (MIGs), including chairs, meeting cadence, and subscription information. Active groups span: Competencies/Credentials/Curriculum SIG, Design for Learning SIG, Tools SIG, Higher Education MIG, Government/Military MIG, pK-12 MIG, Workforce MIG, and Globalisation/Localisation SIG.
Open participation — the primary roster of active community leaders in learning engineering practice. SIGs and MIGs are the working layer where ICICLE standards, frameworks, and white papers are actually drafted; joining a SIG is the most direct path to participating in the field's institutional development.
notes: Open participation; primary community organizing layer for LE standards and practice work.

name: LIVE Learning Innovation Incubator — Vanderbilt Peabody
description:
Peabody College incubator supporting interdisciplinary learning innovation at Vanderbilt University. Brings together researchers, designers, and technologists to develop and evaluate novel learning systems, with emphasis on evidence-based design and emerging AI applications.
Vanderbilt LIVE teams participate in national AI-for-learning efforts, including AI-ALOE partner work on intelligent text and workplace learning tools. The incubator model — rapid prototyping with embedded evaluation — exemplifies the learning engineering design cycle applied at a research university.
notes: AI-ALOE consortium partner; Peabody College, Vanderbilt.

name: National AI Institute for Adult Learning and Online Education (AI-ALOE)
description:
NSF-funded National AI Institute headquartered at Georgia Tech, developing AI theories and systems for adult online learning, intelligent tutors, and workforce upskilling. Consortium includes Georgia Tech, Vanderbilt, Harvard, and other partners. Pursues both foundational AI research and applied system development for real-world adult learners.
Primary Georgia Tech anchor for AI×learning engineering at scale. AI-ALOE represents one of the largest federal investments in AI-driven learning engineering, with particular focus on underserved adult learners and the intersection of workforce development with adaptive educational technology.
notes: NSF National AI Institute; Georgia Tech-led consortium; focus on adult online learning and workforce upskilling.

name: International Society for the Learning Sciences (ISLS)
description:
Academic society organizing the annual ICLS (International Conference of the Learning Sciences) and CSCL (Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning) conferences. Bridges learning science theory and instructional design practice. Emphasizes socio-cultural and humanistic perspectives alongside computational approaches.
Essential for grounding algorithmic LE systems in rich theories of how people learn in social and institutional contexts. ISLS conferences are where learning scientists and education researchers most directly interrogate the assumptions embedded in AI and data-driven learning systems.
notes: Organizes ICLS and CSCL conferences; the primary society for learning sciences as an academic discipline.

name: Human Factors and Ergonomics Society (HFES)
description:
Professional society for human factors and ergonomics. Publishes the Human Factors journal and organizes the annual HFES conference. The Training Systems technical group directly addresses LE-relevant human factors in instructional systems design, including workload, situation awareness, and performance measurement in training environments.
Key community for practitioners designing learning systems in complex, safety-critical environments — military, aviation, nuclear, and healthcare. Human factors methods (task analysis, cognitive workload assessment, human-in-the-loop testing) are essential LE tools when learning outcomes must be validated against real operational performance.
notes: Training Systems technical group is the most directly LE-relevant subgroup.

name: INCOSE Systems Engineering Body of Knowledge (SEBoK)
affiliation_or_venue: INCOSE — International Council on Systems Engineering
description:
Open wiki-based body of knowledge for systems engineering, maintained by INCOSE. Includes the Human Systems Integration knowledge area. Covers SE process frameworks: requirements engineering, architecture, integration, verification and validation, and lifecycle management.
The SE process framework that SEBoK codifies is the engineering process backbone that learning engineering adapts for learning system development. Understanding SE fundamentals — especially requirements traceability and V&V — is essential for LE practitioners working at the boundary of instructional design and systems engineering.
notes: Open wiki; Human Systems Integration knowledge area is most directly LE-relevant.

name: Naturalistic Decision Making Association (NDM)
description:
Research community studying expert decision-making under realistic, complex conditions. NDM methods — critical decision method (CDM), cognitive task analysis (CTA), recognition-primed decision modeling — are the primary toolkit for eliciting tacit expert knowledge in high-stakes training domains.
Key source of needs analysis methods for learning engineering in professional training contexts: military, healthcare, aviation, firefighting, and emergency management. When LE practitioners must translate expert performance into instructional objectives, NDM methods provide the validated approach for surfacing knowledge that experts cannot articulate unprompted.
notes: NDM methods (CDM, CTA) are the standard toolkit for expert knowledge elicitation in high-stakes LE contexts.

name: Credential Engine / CTDL
description:
Open-source data standard and registry for describing credentials, competencies, and learning pathways. The Credential Transparency Description Language (CTDL) encodes 1,200+ properties for representing what people know and can do, enabling machine-readable linkages between learning experiences, competencies, and credentials across providers and platforms.
The emerging national infrastructure for credential-level knowledge representation in competency-based learning engineering systems. For LE practitioners designing workforce-aligned learning systems, CTDL provides the shared ontology needed to map learning outcomes to job requirements and to stack credentials into coherent pathways at scale.
notes: CTDL is the primary open standard for machine-readable credential and competency representation in the US.
